critic_raw:
YNet's two-stage architecture — first predicting a probability distribution over goal locations, then predicting waypoints conditioned on the sampled goal — creates a compounding error problem that the evaluation design does not adequately characterize. If the goal prediction is wrong, all subsequent waypoint predictions will be conditioned on an incorrect goal. The reported trajectory metrics (e.g., Average Displacement Error, Final Displacement Error) are computed over the best prediction from K samples — a practice called 'minimum over K' that masks goal prediction failure. A model that samples diverse goals (including the correct one) will appear to perform well on minADE/minFDE even if its goal prediction is systematically biased toward wrong modes for the particular pedestrian.

Segmentation maps are used as environmental context, but the quality and resolution of segmentation maps on the test set is never characterized. SDD and ETH/UCY datasets have different camera perspectives, scene complexities, and segmentation annotation quality. If YNet's performance advantage comes primarily from scenes where segmentation is highly accurate (e.g., clear walkway boundaries), and degrades to baseline performance on scenes with poor segmentation quality, the aggregated metric hides this conditional performance pattern.

The epistemic/aleatoric decomposition claim — that the model separately handles pedestrian intent (epistemic) and environmental unpredictability (aleatoric) — is architecturally unmotivated. The U-Net subnetworks are not designed with separate epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty heads; the uncertainty decomposition is a post-hoc narrative applied to an architecture that does not structurally separate these sources. The paper conflates architectural design description with theoretical justification.

The multi-modal prediction capability (generating diverse trajectory samples) is evaluated only via best-of-K metrics, not via calibration of the predicted probability distribution. A model that places uniform probability mass over all trajectories would score identically on minADE/minFDE as one with a perfectly calibrated distribution, as long as both include the ground truth trajectory within their K samples. The paper never evaluates whether the predicted probability maps are well-calibrated against the empirical distribution of trajectories.
defender_raw:
YNet's two-stage prediction architecture (goal prediction followed by waypoint-conditioned path prediction) is principled and well-motivated. Trajectory prediction involves two distinct sources of uncertainty: epistemic uncertainty about the pedestrian's intent (where they are going) and aleatoric uncertainty about how they navigate to that goal. Separating these into explicit sub-problems — first predict a distribution over goals, then predict paths conditioned on sampled goals — allows the model to represent and propagate uncertainty in a structured way rather than collapsing both sources into a single opaque distribution.

The use of segmentation maps as environmental context is a sound design choice. Pedestrian trajectories are heavily constrained by physical obstacles and navigable areas. Encoding this information through semantic segmentation (which distinguishes walkable vs. non-walkable areas) provides structured environmental knowledge that is more informative than raw pixels and avoids the model learning to parse scene structure from scratch.

The U-Net backbone for the encoder-decoder sub-networks is well-chosen. U-Net's skip connections preserve spatial resolution across scales, which is important for trajectory heatmap prediction — the model needs to maintain spatial awareness at both coarse (goal region) and fine (exact path) scales. A purely convolutional or purely global architecture would lose one or the other.

The heatmap representation for trajectory probability distributions is more expressive than parametric distributions (e.g., bivariate Gaussians) because it can represent multi-modal distributions without assuming a specific shape. This is important for pedestrian trajectory prediction, where multiple plausible paths may exist and the distribution is often non-Gaussian.

Validation on both SDD and ETH/UCY is appropriate because these datasets have complementary characteristics: ETH/UCY is a standard indoor/outdoor pedestrian benchmark while SDD includes drone footage with diverse scene types. Using both avoids dataset-specific overfitting.

An anticipated critique is that the goal prediction stage introduces a discrete sampling step that may miss low-probability but important trajectories. The defense is that this is explicitly addressed by the multi-modal nature of the goal distribution — multiple goal samples can be drawn, and the model is evaluated in a multi-sample setting consistent with prior work.
all_issues_raised: Two-stage compounding error from goal prediction to waypoint prediction is masked by best-of-K (minADE/minFDE) evaluation — a model that always samples one correct goal among K will appear accurate even if its goal prediction is systematically wrong for the specific pedestrian's mode.
Segmentation map quality varies across SDD and ETH/UCY scenes and is never characterized — performance may be conditioned on segmentation accuracy in ways the aggregate metric hides.

The epistemic/aleatoric decomposition claim is architecturally unmotivated — the U-Net subnetworks do not structurally separate these uncertainty sources, making the decomposition narrative a post-hoc label applied to an undifferentiated architecture.

Multi-modal prediction is evaluated only via best-of-K metrics, not distribution calibration — a model with poorly calibrated probability mass over trajectories scores identically to a well-calibrated one on minADE/minFDE, so the quality of the predictive distribution is never assessed.
all_issues_adjudicated: Two-stage compounding error from goal prediction to waypoint prediction is masked by best-of-K (minADE/minFDE) evaluation — a model that always samples one correct goal among K will appear accurate even if its goal prediction is systematically wrong for the specific pedestrian's mode.
Segmentation map quality varies across SDD and ETH/UCY scenes and is never characterized — performance may be conditioned on segmentation accuracy in ways the aggregate metric hides.

The epistemic/aleatoric decomposition claim is architecturally unmotivated — the U-Net subnetworks do not structurally separate these uncertainty sources, making the decomposition narrative a post-hoc label applied to an undifferentiated architecture.

Multi-modal prediction is evaluated only via best-of-K metrics, not distribution calibration — a model with poorly calibrated probability mass over trajectories scores identically to a well-calibrated one on minADE/minFDE, so the quality of the predictive distribution is never assessed.
